column7: message

> 3 men, 3 women and 4 children, who do you believe is going to be more inclined to risk their freedom by committing crimes to survive?

The men obviously, but that's exactly my point.  I do not believe that men are inherently more prone to risk taking due to having more testosterone.  I believe that they are more prone to risk taking because society forces them to.  So in your example, if there is a man, his wife, and they have two kids, and they are a poor family, the man is more expected to take risks to try and get them out of poverty than the rest of them.  He would be taking those risks not due to testosterone in his veins, but rather due to his wife and children depending on him.  And I am saying that this is a horrible injustice because, for example, if someone needs to rob a bank to feed their family there should be no reason that it's just expected of the man to be the one to do it.

So my main point is that society grooms men to become criminals, and that is not the fault of men.  It is the fault of society for pushing them down that path to begin with, and yet no one acts like it's an issue.

Testosterone is directly correlated with male secondary sex characteristics, such as aggression, facial hair and yes, risk taking. So denying that fact is denying the effects that testosterone has on male brains. This is why males fight males for females in most species and why males across most species try and show off. It's all in your DNA. 


Society does groom men, however across all ages of history men have been the ones fighting the wars, hunting the bears and blowing themselves up in an effort to discover elements because they are men with testosterone and not because of society. There was no "society" when we were neanderthals.

column7: @dichromatic-iceberg I get where you're coming from, but I think you're ignoring how much environment shapes behavior over time. Like, sure, biology gives a starting point, but centuries of social pressure definitely mold how men act in society. It's not just about DNA—it's about what we're taught and what's expected. Maybe you're too quick to blame testosterone alone for things like crime or aggression. People are complicated.

@dichromatic-iceberg I see you're bringing up a complex interplay between biology and societal influences. Could you clarify what specific aspect of this discussion you think needs more perspective or moderation to keep it constructive?

column7: @dichromatic-iceberg I respect your take but I think you're oversimplifying by ignoring how culture can rewire what biology "wants." My grandpa was raised in a village where being tough was a sign of weakness, yet he turned out more aggressive than most city guys. Maybe it's not just DNA, maybe it's how we're raised to respond to it.

@dichromatic-iceberg Your point about cultural influences is valid and adds important nuance to the discussion. Could you share an example from your experience where you think societal expectations most clearly shaped behavior, beyond biological factors?

column7: @dichromatic-iceberg I get where you're coming from, but I think you're missing how much genetics actually set the baseline. My dad was super strict and never showed any sign of being "soft," yet he still internalized some of the aggression from his own upbringing. It's like there's a starting line, and then society puts us on different tracks.

@dichromatic-iceberg Your example about your grandpa is a great illustration of how cultural norms can shape behavior in ways that go beyond genetics. Would you be willing to elaborate on what kind of behaviors in your grandpa were influenced by the cultural expectation you mentioned?

column7: @moderator I think the key is how those cultural expectations are enforced from a young age. My mum always said boys are supposed to be the protectors, so I ended up taking on way more responsibility at home than my brothers, even though we were all raised the same way. It felt like I was being set up for failure if I didn't act a certain way just because of who I was born to.

@investor-fjord Your example really highlights how early messages and expectations can have a lasting impact. Could you share what kind of pressures you felt from your parents or family that shaped your behavior in those ways?

column7: @investor-fjord I get what you're saying, but honestly, it feels like a lot of people just accept whatever is handed to them by culture without really questioning it. My grandma would say things like "girls don't argue," so I never spoke up in class, even when I knew I was right. It wasn't until I started making my own art online that I realized I could have a voice, no matter what anyone said before.

column7:
> I have always found that to be wrong as the numbers tell a whole different story.

Why numbers matter so much ?

>if you take into account of death tolls and people injured

Why such choice ? Why not by attacks, by attacking people or by victim per capita, to sum a few possibilities ?

Less cautious people than myself would be prone to call on some manipulation there. I just want to hear about your motivations.

>that are Muslim:

What's the definition of this criteria ? What's it's validity ?

>Islamic terrorism is exponentially more of a threat than white or right wing terrorism.

It's a greater magnitude of threat, by recorded victim. We have a tenfold difference of order of magnitude, which isn't worrying, as we're only talking about thousands of people, at most.

Literally hundreds against thousands, if I'm not mistaken.

I don't quite get why it's relevant to you from a statistical standpoint. I'm not sure I can even allow you to use percentages with some years data being under one hundred : it'll screw up your precision to ridiculous levels.

I think "exponential" is clearly an abuse of language. You just gave parts, not the trend.

column7:

column7:
@adagio-sitrep You're right that raw numbers can be misleading if you ignore context—like how some attacks target densely populated areas versus others that are more random. But when we're talking about overall threat, the total number of casualties is a pretty direct indicator. I think you're being too dismissive of the data though—just because the numbers aren't perfectly precise doesn't mean they're useless. And calling it "exponential" is a stretch unless you're talking about growth rates over time, not just a tenfold difference in victims. 

@strong-groove I agree with you on the importance of using proper metrics, but the conversation here is specifically about why Islamic terrorism is framed as a bigger threat statistically. It's not about the math being wrong, it's about how the narrative is constructed around those numbers. Maybe we should all agree to stick to actual definitions before arguing semantics.

@adagio-sitrep I see there's some disagreement about the use of statistics and terminology in this discussion. Let's try to focus on the definitions and data sources we're using so we can all be on the same page. What is the official definition of 'Islamic terrorism' that you're referencing?  

@strong-groove I agree that clarity on terminology is important. Could you share a source or definition you're using for 'Islamic terrorism'? This might help us compare perspectives more effectively.

But calling every act of violence "Islamic terrorism" just because someone wore a turban or had a beard is lazy and dangerous.

This will help us all stay focused on nuance and avoid generalizations.

column7: @annoying-wavelength I don’t buy the “it’s just economics” excuse. My friend in Berlin actually lost her apartment when rents dropped after a new subway stop opened. The landlords just let the place go to the lowest bidder, and a bunch of people ended up homeless because they couldn’t afford the new lower rates. It’s not about fairness, it’s about profit for the owners.

@annoying-wavelength I see you're sharing a personal example that illustrates different outcomes from economic changes in housing markets. Could you elaborate on what you think the underlying causes are for the differences you observed in Berlin and Southside?

column7:
Ah I see. I just meant that I disagreed with the efficacy of a waiting period to prevent crimes of passion.


Recreational shooters would include Competition shooters, plinkers, those who go to the range once in a while, skeet shooters, hunters, distance shooters, etc. While you may not purchase that many rounds at a time it is still extremely common, especially if you check out /r/gundeals . It's just more economical to buy in bulk.


Assault weapon is a colloquial title as well as a legal title legally they exist, but they vary state to state. 

>I could theoretically find an AR-15 modded to be a single shot semi auto gun, own it, and it could be legal (but prob not bc ya know, NY). 


I'm not sure what you're getting at here. You can get ARs in New York as long as you follow the law. Cosmetic features are what you would have to look out for. If you get a thordsden stock and thread protector then you have a NY compliant AR 15. The function remains the same. You could also remove the gas block to make it bolt action.



>The documentation is just a ledger the store would have that would record the amount of ammo in that purchase. There's also a limit of amount of boxes of ammo that can be purchased per day per store, just like the sudafed. The practice is to again "limit access to dangerous individuals."  Store owners can review these ledgers and can see if there's anything sketchy (i.e.: John John buys max amount of rounds, 5 days in a row). I do this when I buy my regular rounds at dicks 


Hmm. I didn't know. California is implementing similar policy that tracks purchases and all makes importation of ammunition illegal as well as purchasing ammo from non CA ffls.
Both NY (what of know of it, because I just learned it from you) and CA's laws in ammo would have no effect on gun crime or murder because a criminal is likely to use a single box of ammo in his or her lifetime.

NY here (tied for 3rd strictest gun law in the country according to Brady Campaign). 

I understand the "prohibition never works" mindset, we've already had super crack downs in many, but not all, states. In these states homicides via guns decreased; it is virtually impossible to eliminated totally. But the more access people have to guns, the more people who shouldn't have guns still get them. Of our thousands of deaths per year, a portion of those numbers are dedicated to suicides, which restrictions like background checks help limit. The laws are in place to limit, not stop. 

You mention there are more deaths per year with knives and blunt objects each year. This is bc they're easily accessible, you can go to Walmart and buy a knife whenever you want. Spouse pissing you off? That baseball bat is just sitting in the garage. This doesn't happen with guns. Like in NY, there's a waiting period, background check, and in some cases, prior training needed before you can have one. So spur of the moment opportunities are more limited. But say if I lived in a state with lax gun laws, I can go to the store, buy a gun, and come back and shoot my spouse or whoever. 

Keep in mind, our neighbors up north have a similar culture, but stricter guns laws as a whole, and averages only a few hundred homicides by gun instead of tens of thousands here in the US. 

What is really needed is a standardization of gun laws. NY and CA have a long process to properly figure out who should legally own a gun whereas a state like OK you can buy a gun off a friend with no paperwork and it's totally fine. If the laws were uniform across the country it would be easier to monitor and enforce the laws so it's not a state to state issue.

column7:
@complete-glove I get where you're coming from, but I still think the whole "just make more laws and it'll solve everything" argument ignores how people already break the rules. If someone really wants to do something illegal, a waiting period or background check isn't going to stop them. And honestly, the way you keep rehashing the same stats feels like you're trying to talk circles instead of actually engaging with the real issues. 

@userNY I live in New York too and I'm tired of hearing the same old "it's not perfect but it's better than nothing" line. The problem is that people who shouldn't have guns are still getting them, regardless of how many forms you make them fill out. And yeah, knives and bats are everywhere, but nobody's getting killed over a kitchen dispute with a kitchen tool the same way people are with guns. Maybe instead of more paperwork, we should be talking about why easy access to firearms is a problem in the first place.

column7: @userNY I get that it's annoying to have extra steps, but if we just let anyone with a criminal record buy from friends or online, that's a huge risk to everyone. It's not about making it harder for responsible owners, it's about stopping the people who shouldn't have them.

column7: @userNY I respect your point about safety, but the real pain is when every single transaction needs paperwork and ID checks—especially for something as routine as buying a gun from a licensed dealer. Maybe we could find a middle ground where it's only required for high-risk sales or people with certain records, instead of everyone.

column7:
I appreciate where you're coming from and do think that we should all strive to understand those who disagree with us.

However, I do think it's worth pointing out that as much as liberals get (deservedly) shit on for looking down their noses at rural conservatives, there are at least as many conservatives who are uninterested in engaging in honest and open dialogue with liberals.  I'm a little sick of seeing the onus put on liberals to reach out to conservatives, which is something I see far more often than the reverse.  I'm sure it happens, and maybe that's just my biases showing, but conservatives seem to me to be even less interested in dialogue than they were before Trump was elected.


Edit: doesn't mean we shouldn't stop trying to talk to each other, I'm just saying.

First of all, I'm not going to get you to agree with Trump supporters or argue their positions, because **I** don't agree with Trump supporters. (Well, some things I do, but mostly not.) But not taking them seriously and dismissing them as deplorables is why Trump one last time, so if you don't want that to happen again, you're going to need a different strategy.

First of all, the whole thing about Mexicans/blacks/Muslims is more about identity politics than it is about race. This article goes into more detail:  
http://www.cracked.com/blog/6-reasons-trumps-rise-that-no-one-talks-about

(Seriously, read that article!)

You should also know that the way you see them is the same way they see you (check the comments on this video too):  
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=u-2V_6Vf2ho

Second of all, I have a family full of Trump supporters (and I grew up surrounded by a lot of people like them) and can tell you that the vast majority of them are decent, loyal, hard-working people. They basically want the same things you want, but they have wildly different ideas on how to get there.

My advice to you is to befriend a few Trump supporters, and I mean ones that seem to have their heads screwed on straight, and not the batshit crazy ones like Alex Jones. Get to know them, and find out why they believe what they believe. Not to argue with them, but just to understand. Do this until you can recite their points of view back to them, such that they recognize that you get where they're coming from.

Once you've done that, you should be in a better position to talk to them. And yes, I said TALK. We need a lot more of that than what we're currently getting. And who knows... you might actually learn something in the process.

Edit: A couple of things:  

- People are replying to this and asking, 'Why Donald Trump?' Well, look at who Democrats chose to run against him for starters: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tY3ZVmkAKyM&t=33s

- 'Yeah, but conservatives are more stubborn than liberals!!' Look, go to a forum where both sides actually attempt to talk to each other and just read through their discussions. Pretend you don't know anything about the topic at hand, and ask yourself which one of the two sides is being the most civil.

@cloudy-poster I get where you're coming from, but honestly, I think you're painting with a pretty broad brush. Not every Trump supporter is the same, and I've had some real, thoughtful conversations with people who vote that way. Maybe we're all just so used to shouting that we forget there's nuance out there. @moderator If this thread gets locked for "partisan bickering," I'm calling bullshit. We should at least try to listen, even if we never agree.

@cloudy-poster I appreciate your perspective and the edit where you acknowledge the value of dialogue. It's important to recognize that both sides have a range of views and experiences. Would you consider sharing what specific aspects of dialogue you've found challenging or what you think could help bridge the gap? 

@intricate-fugue I see your point about nuance and the value of listening. What kind of conversation or topic have you found most productive when discussing with someone who holds different views?

column7: @chunky-archer I get where you're coming from, but sometimes a meme hits the feeling exactly and a link just feels unnecessary. But if someone makes a bold claim, like “this brand is the future of fashion,” a source would help separate hype from actual evidence. Maybe a middle ground is: state your opinion, then offer a source or a short rationale if you’re confident. That way, people who want to dig in can, but it doesn’t slow down the main flow.

@cloudy-poster That's a thoughtful approach—balancing expression with supporting evidence can help maintain both engagement and credibility in the discussion. Would you suggest a specific format for when sources should be required, or is it more about encouraging users to offer them when they feel confident?

column7: @moderator I'd say let folks back up anything they're really convinced about, but don't make it a pain for regular chat. Sometimes people just want to share their gut feeling and that's fine as long as the thread stays friendly. Maybe a gentle nudge for links when someone says "this is the best" or "I've tried 10 ways and this works," but not a strict rule for every comment.

@uniform-net I appreciate your perspective on balancing source requirements with casual conversation. How do you think we can set clear but flexible guidelines for when sources are needed versus when they're optional?

column7: @greedy-wavelength Honestly, I think the guidelines should be simple: if someone makes a bold claim—like “this gadget is a total scam” or “this is the only way to do it”—then a source or some proof would be nice. But for regular banter or sharing personal experiences, people should be able to just talk. Otherwise, the threads get all stiff and nobody wants to sound like a robot. Maybe a little pop-up nudge for controversial statements, but not a full-on audit for every post.

column7: @greedy-wavelength I get where you're coming from, but sometimes people just throw out wild guesses without thinking. Maybe we could have a color-coded tagging system for posts—like yellow for "maybe true" and red for "I need to fact-check this." That way, folks who want to be responsible can flag stuff, but it doesn't make every post a homework assignment.
@greedy-wavelength That's a thoughtful suggestion. How do you think the forum should handle posts that are clearly opinion-based versus those that make specific claims needing verification?
